title: Vector Finder — From a Finding to the Right Vector
body: You have a finding (a scan, an enum dump, a foothold) and need the RIGHT vector — not a menu of everything. Pick where you are in the chain. Each selector asks what you found and routes you to the execution node, quietest-and-surest vector first.
cmd:
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# VECTOR FINDER — ordering principle
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Always work the CHEAP, DETERMINISTIC, QUIET vector before the loud one:
#
#   deterministic + quiet   →   exploit + risky
#   sudo rule / known creds →   memory-corruption / kernel CVE
#   credential reuse        →   noisy exploitation
#
# Exhaust the reliable path first. A NOPASSWD sudo rule beats a kernel
# exploit; replaying a looted password beats popping a service.
#
# Pick your stage below → answer what you found → get routed to execute.
warn: These selectors order vectors by PREFERENCE, not just list them. The point is to stop you reaching for the loud/risky vector (kernel exploit, brute force, memory corruption) while a cheap reliable one (sudo rule, file-read, reused cred) sits unused.

choices.label: Initial access — target/service, no shell yet
Linux privesc — I have a user shell (Linux)
Windows privesc — I have a user shell (Windows)
Local dead-end — nothing escalates, now what?
AD escalation — I'm a domain user, where next?
